text: The surgical team sets up the instruments, attaching five-millimeter allied medical ports to the robotic arms, preparing for the procedure.
The surgical setup includes a 10-millimeter, 30-degree down, 3D camera positioned for visualizing the surgical field.

The surgeon adjusts the robotic arms within the ports, performing port training to ensure precise positioning of the arms as they enter the skin. The next versatile arm is being maneuvered into place.
The surgeon inserts a 5mm Maryland Grasper through the anterior port into the chest, completing the setup with three robotic arms and a 10mm 3D endoscopic camera for the thymectomy procedure.
The surgeon uses the third arm to pull the middle lobe of the lung posteriorly, revealing the anterior hilum. The tumor is visible as the lung is manipulated.
The surgeon has a clear view of the phrenic nerve and begins dissection in the surgical field, utilizing the endoscopic camera for enhanced visualization.

The surgeon uses a hook diathermy to perform precise dissection, manipulating tissue with the hook instrument. The visual focus is on the controlled application of the diathermy tool during the procedure.
The surgeon uses a Maryland dissector with bipolar energy on the left side, with options to switch to a spatula or scissors that also have energy capabilities.

The surgeon uses a surgical instrument to lift the middle lobe of the lung, while maintaining CO₂ insufflation in the chest at six millimeters of mercury. Small areas of bleeding are wiped away using gauze.
The surgeon exposes the middle lobe vein and proceeds to locate the middle lobe bronchus. A sizable lymph node is visible adjacent to the middle lobe.

The surgeon uses robotic arms to dissect the junction between the middle lobe vein and the middle lobe bronchus, with the third arm retracting the bronchus located in front of the pulmonary artery (PA).

The camera zooms in to reveal the middle lobe bronchus centrally located, with a lymph node obscuring the pulmonary artery and its branches. The surgeon uses two Maryland forceps to perform blunt dissection around the middle lobe vein, utilizing the delicate 5mm instruments for precise maneuvering.

The surgeon uses an eight millimeter Covidien curve tip stapler with fifty degrees of articulation to pair with the middle lobe vein, demonstrating the compatibility of the stapler's size with the vein.

The surgeon moves around the middle lobe bronchus, with a lymph node visible in the field. Sven uses bilateral blunt dissection to carefully navigate around both sides of the bronchus, employing his third arm for lifting to enhance bimanual dexterity.

The surgeon navigates around the middle lobe bronchus using a sling for better access. The camera provides a panoramic view showing the lower lobe, pericardium, and diaphragm. The camera is positioned in the ninth intercostal space at the midline, with an additional port on the left side in the eighth space.

The surgical team positions ports approximately six to eight centimeters lateral to the spine, with right-handed ports at the eighth intercostal space and a high midline port for the third arm. The team identifies a prominent middle lobe pulmonary artery and decides to address it first, facilitating easier stapling of the bronchus.

The surgeon uses an 8mm Cavidian White stapler to place two lines of staples on either side of the middle lobe artery. The third robotic arm is visible, lifting the tissue to facilitate the stapling process.

The surgeon performs a lymph node dissection using a Maryland bipolar instrument, carefully excising the lymph node. A finger of a glove is used to assist in the removal, and the lymph node is extracted through the assistant port.

The surgeon uses bipolar Maryland forceps to dissect behind the middle lobe bronchus, revealing a second segmental pulmonary artery. The instruments maneuver around the artery, which appears similar in size to the vein, providing a clear view from below. The delicate grasp of the instruments aids in handling the lung tissue without causing damage.

The surgeon carefully closes the jaws of the robotic instrument around the last segmental artery, ensuring the staple is positioned correctly under direct vision. The robotic approach allows for a view from below, facilitating access to both anterior and posterior aspects, including station seven and the anterior hilum.
The surgeon completes the dissection of the fissure using robotic instruments inserted through small five-millimeter ports, which are visibly smaller than typical VATS multiport incisions.
The surgeon uses a Covidien tri stapler with a 60 ml purple load to perform the final stapling, completing the lobectomy. The surgical team positions the instruments and tissues carefully.

The surgeon uses a purple stapler to perform a firing on the middle lobe, effectively completing the lobectomy. The assistant uses scissors to finalize the procedure by removing any remaining tissue stumps.
